comments:
Summary:
The manuscript develops a framework for kernel-based adaptive filtering of complex-valued signals. The main technical contributions are (i) a technique to ‘‘complexify’’ real RKHSs so that common real kernels can be used with complex inputs, (ii) promotion and use of the complex Gaussian kernel, (iii) an extension of Wirtinger’s calculus (W- and CW-derivatives) to general complex (possibly infinite-dimensional) RKHSs via Fréchet derivatives, and (iv) two variants of a Complex Kernel LMS (CKLMS) algorithm (one using complexified real kernels, one using complex kernels). Experiments on a nonlinear channel-equalization task show substantial steady-state MSE improvements over standard complex LMS and widely-linear CLMS.

Strengths:
- The paper addresses a clear gap: most kernel methods have been applied to real-valued data; treating complex-valued signals in an RKHS setting is practically important (e.g., communications). 
- The extension of Wirtinger calculus to general Hilbert spaces (via Fréchet derivatives) is a natural and useful theoretical tool for deriving adaptive algorithms operating in complex RKHSs; the paper provides definitions, key properties, and several useful identities. 
- The distinction between using pure complex kernels and complexification of real kernels is clearly explained; providing both CKLMS1 and CKLMS2 is helpful. 
- The algorithms are practical (instantaneous updates, normalized versions, and a simple sparsification / novelty criterion). The experiments demonstrate clear empirical gains in the tested scenario.

Weaknesses and points for improvement:
- Experimental evaluation is somewhat limited: a single application scenario (one nonlinear channel-equalization model) and a single dataset size (5000 samples). It would strengthen the manuscript to include more tasks (other nonlinearities, different SNRs, different degrees of input circularity), sensitivity to kernel parameters (σ), and computational/complexity comparisons (runtime, dictionary sizes, memory) versus baselines. 
- The comparison set is narrow: the paper compares against CLMS and WL-CLMS (both linear). There is limited comparison to other nonlinear or kernel-based complex processing alternatives (for example, alternative ways of handling real/imag pairs using real RKHSs, or any prior kernelized complex methods if any). This weakens the claim of superiority in more general contexts. 
- Some theoretical claims are stated at a high level with proofs deferred or sketched; while many arguments are plausible (and appendices contain proofs for several propositions), some parts could benefit from more rigorous conditions (e.g., precise function-class/regularity assumptions under which the Fréchet-Wirtinger Taylor expansions hold, and convergence analysis of CKLMS variants). In particular, claims such as ‘‘important properties of LMS carry over to CKLMS’’ are asserted but not fully proved or quantified (e.g., mean convergence conditions, step-size bounds, misadjustment expressions). 
- The relationship and practical differences between the two CKLMS formulations (the two ways of writing the instantaneous output and their consequences) are discussed but could be clarified further, with guidelines on which formulation to use in practice. 
- Sparsification uses the novelty criterion only; a short discussion or small study comparing other sparsification/budget methods (coherence criterion, approximate linear dependence, budgeted approaches) would be valuable for practical deployment.

Minor/Presentation remarks:
- The manuscript is dense but generally well organized. Some parts are algebra-heavy; adding a small intuitive paragraph after key derivations (e.g., role of the h and h* terms in the Hilbert-Wirtinger expansion) would help readers. 
- The notation is mostly consistent; ensure that the distinction between maps Φ (real kernel feature map) and Φ̃ (complex kernel feature map) is always explicit to avoid reader confusion. 

Overall assessment and recommendation:
This paper makes a novel and useful contribution: extending Wirtinger calculus to RKHSs and proposing practical CKLMS algorithms that enable kernel adaptive filtering for complex signals. The theoretical development is solid in its main lines and the empirical results are promising. The paper would be stronger with broader experiments and somewhat more complete theoretical statements about convergence, but as it stands it is a worthwhile contribution that should interest researchers in signal processing and kernel methods. I recommend acceptance/posting with the suggested improvements (expanded experiments, added discussion of convergence conditions and sparsification alternatives).
